content.text: TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript that adds a static type system. The TypeScript compiler (tsc) type-checks your code and emits plain JavaScript that any runtime can execute. Before you can write TypeScript, you need Node.js installed and a way to invoke tsc.

attrs.alt: TypeScript toolchain: source files → tsc compiler → JavaScript → runtime; three installation options

content.text: The diagram shows the two-step flow: you write .ts files, tsc compiles them to .js, and the JavaScript runs in the browser or Node.js. Three installation strategies are shown at the bottom — local project installs are recommended for all serious work.
— TypeScript is distributed as an npm package. Download from nodejs.org or install via nvm (
— bash, zsh, PowerShell, or Windows Terminal all work.

# Verify prerequisites
node --version    # v20.x.x or v22.x.x
npm --version     # 9.x.x or 10.x.x
command available everywhere on your machine without needing
. Use it for one-off scripts or to explore TypeScript outside a project:

npm install -g typescript

# Verify
tsc --version     # Version 5.x.x

Global installs create a version coupling between all your projects — if one project needs TypeScript 4.x and another needs 5.x, a global install can only satisfy one at a time. Prefer local installs for team projects.
A local install pins the TypeScript version to package.json, so every contributor on the team uses the same compiler version regardless of what is installed globally on their machines.

mkdir my-ts-project && cd my-ts-project
npm init -y
npm install --save-dev typescript

# Check version without a global install:
npx tsc --version    # Version 5.x.x
in package.json. It is a build tool, not a runtime dependency, so it belongs in
file. This file tells the compiler which files to compile, what JavaScript version to emit, and which type-checking rules to enforce:
with every option listed as a comment. The defaults are sensible for most projects. The key fields to understand immediately:
on from the start. Turning it off is much harder later when you have a large codebase that depends on the relaxed rules.

npx tsc          # compiles src/index.ts → dist/index.js
node dist/index.js  # Hello, TypeScript!
If you introduce a type error, tsc prints the file, line number, and error code but still emits JavaScript by default. Use the noEmitOnError compiler option to prevent emitting when there are errors:
once compiles and exits. For development, use watch mode so the compiler re-runs automatically every time you save a file:

npx tsc --watch
# or the shorthand:
npx tsc -w
Watch mode is useful when working on standalone scripts. In Angular projects the Angular CLI handles watching internally — you do not need to run tsc --watch yourself.
For quick experiments or Node.js scripts you can run TypeScript directly without a separate compile step, using one of several tools:
compiles each file on the fly using the TypeScript compiler API. It respects your tsconfig.json. Slower than tsx for large files because it does full type-checking.
uses esbuild under the hood for very fast TypeScript execution. It strips types rather than type-checking, so it is faster but will not catch type errors. Use it for development scripts.

# Deno is a separate runtime — install from deno.land
deno run src/index.ts

Deno is a TypeScript-first runtime that supports .ts files natively. It has a different module system (URL imports) and security model (explicit permission flags) from Node.js. It is not used for Angular projects, but is worth knowing for standalone TypeScript work.
Angular CLI projects come with TypeScript pre-configured. When you run ng new my-app:
as a local devDependency — the exact version is chosen by the CLI to match the Angular version.
extend the root tsconfig — app for the production build and spec for unit tests.
The Angular compiler (ngcc / esbuild + swc) wraps tsc
— you never invoke tsc directly for builds. Use

ng new my-app
cd my-app
cat package.json | grep typescript    # "typescript": "~5.4.0"
cat tsconfig.json                     # Angular-tailored settings

Do not change the TypeScript version in an Angular project manually. The Angular team tests each release against specific TypeScript versions, and mismatches can cause compilation errors. Use ng update to upgrade both Angular and TypeScript together.
The Angular compatibility matrix is published on angular.dev. Stick to the supported range — TypeScript versions outside it are blocked by the Angular compiler with a clear error message.

# Minimal smoke test — should print Version 5.x.x
npx tsc --version

# Compile a file and check for errors
echo 'const x: number = "oops"' > /tmp/test.ts
npx tsc --noEmit /tmp/test.ts
# Expected: error TS2322: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'.
rm /tmp/test.ts
Global TypeScript not installed or PATH not updated
tsconfig.json option not recognised by installed TS version
Upgrade TypeScript or remove the unsupported option
Add shorthand scripts to package.json to avoid typing npx tsc each time:

npm run build         # compile once
npm run build:watch   # watch mode
npm run type-check    # check types without emitting JS
script is particularly useful in CI pipelines — it verifies types without producing output files, running faster than a full build.
When you run tsc, the compiler performs four distinct phases in order. Understanding this pipeline helps you interpret error messages and tune performance.

tsc reads every .ts file listed in tsconfig.json (via include/files/rootDir) and parses them into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). Syntax errors — missing brackets, invalid keywords — are caught here. The parser is fast and does not need type information.
The binder walks the AST and builds a symbol table: a map of every identifier (variable, function, class, interface) to its declaration. This is where
scoping rules are enforced and where TypeScript detects duplicate variable declarations.

The type checker resolves types for every expression using the symbol table. This is the slowest phase and the one that produces TypeScript's signature red underlines. The type checker emits no output — it only produces diagnostics.

The emitter transforms the type-annotated AST into plain JavaScript by stripping type annotations, downlevelling syntax (e.g. optional chaining to explicit null checks for older targets), and writing .js and .d.ts declaration files to the outDir.
The --noEmit flag skips Phase 4 entirely. This is useful in CI when you only want to verify types without producing output files.
tsconfig.json has over 100 options. The ones below are the most relevant for Angular and general TypeScript work:
JS version emitted. Use ES2022 for modern Node.js / evergreen browsers.
Emit .d.ts files alongside .js — required for libraries.
Emit .js.map files for debugger source-map support.
Suppress .js output when type errors exist.
Enables the six options below all at once — always use this.
are not assignable to every type.
Function parameter types are checked contravariantly.
Class properties must be initialised in the constructor.
Variables without a type annotation cannot have implicit
inside functions without an explicit type annotation is an error.
is emitted at the top of every output file.
Node.js projects using .mts/.cts files or package.json exports
Projects using a bundler (Webpack/Vite/esbuild) — more permissive
Set the root for non-relative imports (e.g. import from 'src/app/...')
Map import aliases — Angular's tsconfig uses this for @app/* patterns
Large TypeScript codebases (monorepos, Angular libraries) use project references to break the codebase into independently compilable units with incremental builds:

# Build all referenced projects in dependency order
tsc --build

# Build and watch all projects
tsc --build --watch

# Clean all build outputs
tsc --build --clean
in its tsconfig.json and must emit declaration files (
). Angular CLI Workspaces with multiple projects use this pattern internally — each project in angular.json corresponds to a tsconfig that references the root tsconfig.

Declaration files describe the types of a JavaScript library without containing any implementation. When you install a package like lodash and TypeScript reports it has no types, it means no .d.ts file was found.

# Many packages bundle their own types — nothing extra needed:
npm install dayjs        # ships with dayjs/typings/**/*.d.ts

# Others publish types separately via @types:
npm install lodash
npm install --save-dev @types/lodash

# Check if types are bundled:
ls node_modules/dayjs/index.d.ts    # exists → bundled
ls node_modules/lodash/index.d.ts   # may not exist → need @types/lodash
@types/* packages are sourced from the DefinitelyTyped repository — a community-maintained collection of type definitions for thousands of JavaScript libraries. When you run
, you're installing type definitions that describe Node.js's built-in modules.
The TypeScript compiler automatically picks up all @types/* packages in node_modules. You do not need to add them to tsconfig.json unless you want to restrict which globals are available (use the
compiler option to opt in to specific packages only).
For large projects, tsc can cache the previous compilation result and only recheck files that changed. Enable this with
file to disk after each successful compilation. On the next run it reads this file and only reprocesses files whose content or dependencies changed. Add
to .gitignore — it is a build artefact, not source code.
Type-checking is typically added as a CI step that runs before tests and build:

# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: 'npm'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run type-check    # tsc --noEmit
      - run: npm test
      - run: npm run build

as a separate step before the build. This makes type errors immediately visible in the CI log without waiting for the full compilation. The step is fast (under 30 seconds for most projects) because it skips file emission.
Configuring the TypeScript Language Server in Editors

Most modern editors use the TypeScript Language Server Protocol (LSP) to provide IntelliSense, go-to-definition, and inline error display. The LSP reads your tsconfig.json to understand the project structure.
VS Code bundles its own TypeScript version. To use the project's version instead, add to
After setting this, VS Code will prompt you to "Switch TypeScript version" — choose "Use workspace version". This ensures the editor and the command-line compiler behave identically.
Go to Settings → Languages & Frameworks → TypeScript. Set the TypeScript path to
in your project directory. The bundled TypeScript is used only if no project-local TypeScript is found.

# Install typescript-language-server globally (required by most LSP clients)
npm install -g typescript-language-server typescript

# Then configure your LSP client to start typescript-language-server --stdio
# Most configs are in lspconfig (Neovim) or similar plugins
The TypeScript Playground (available at typescriptlang.org/play) is a browser-based editor that runs the TypeScript compiler in WebAssembly. You can:
Write TypeScript and see the compiled JavaScript output side by side.
Hover over any expression to see its inferred type.
Switch between TypeScript versions to test how your code behaves on older releases.
Share snippets via URL — the playground encodes the code in the URL hash.
Enable all strict options or individual options through the Config dropdown — useful for checking how
affects existing code before enabling it in a project.
The Playground is the fastest way to answer 'what does TypeScript do with this type expression?' without setting up a project. Keep a browser tab open to it during learning.
Verifying a Successful Angular TypeScript Setup
After creating an Angular project, use these checks to confirm that TypeScript is configured correctly:

cd my-angular-app

# 1. TypeScript version matches Angular's supported range
npx tsc --version       # should be ~5.4.x for Angular 18

# 2. No type errors in the generated scaffold
npx tsc --noEmit        # should exit with 0 (no output = no errors)

# 3. Strict mode is on
cat tsconfig.json | grep strict   # "strict": true

# 4. VS Code uses the project TypeScript
# Check status bar bottom-right: should say "TypeScript 5.x.x" not "5.x.x (vscode)"

# 5. Angular Language Service works in templates
# Open app.component.html and hover over a binding — should show type info

All five checks should pass on a freshly generated Angular project. If npx tsc --noEmit exits with errors on a new project, the TypeScript version is incompatible with the installed Angular version — run ng update to fix this.
Migrating a JavaScript Project to TypeScript
If you are adding TypeScript to an existing JavaScript project rather than starting fresh, the recommended approach is incremental adoption:
Install TypeScript and create tsconfig.json with
Rename files one by one from .js to .ts, fixing type errors as you go.

option allows .js and .ts files to coexist in the same project. Start by adding type annotations to the most critical files first — the ones with the most callers — so type safety propagates outward through the codebase.

TypeScript is installed as an npm package. For project work always install it locally (npm install --save-dev typescript) and invoke it via npx tsc to ensure the project-pinned version is used. Run npx tsc --init to generate tsconfig.json and keep strict: true enabled from the start. In Angular projects the CLI manages TypeScript installation and configuration — you do not invoke tsc directly, and upgrades happen through ng update.
